food_subgroup: Game meat dishes — stir-fried venison with black pepper sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general deer meat food product class (FOODON_03311737). No specific entry for black pepper deer meat preparation exists; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers deer/venison as a raw ingredient category. No composite dish code for black pepper deer meat exists; closest_parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226955003 identifies venison as a substance. No specific prepared dish code exists; closest_parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of black pepper deer meat is approximately 250 g, comprising sliced venison stir-fried in black pepper sauce with aromatics, served as a main dish alongside rice.

nutrient_source.title: Venison, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 17162); adapted for black pepper stir-fry preparation using USDA data for black pepper sauce components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for black pepper deer meat as a composite dish exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy venison (raw, NDB 17162 / FDC 174299) as the primary protein base, with adjustments for cooking losses (approximately 25% moisture loss on stir-frying) and addition of black pepper sauce components (soy sauce, oyster sauce, oil, black pepper, garlic, onion). Energy and macros reflect the cooked composite dish estimate. Secondary reference: USDA FoodData Central Foundation Foods for soy sauce and black pepper contributions.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; analogous estimate based on cooked meat and low-carbohydrate stir-fry dishes
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for black pepper deer meat. GI is estimated at 15 based on analogous low-carbohydrate cooked meat dishes (e.g., stir-fried beef, GI ~0–20 range) from Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The dish is predominantly protein and fat with minimal carbohydrate (4.2 g per 100 g from sauce components), yielding a very low GI. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (15 × 10.5 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 1.6 GL units.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and oyster sauce (~520 mg per 100 g; ~1300 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sauce preparation, as a standard 250 g serving provides approximately 1300 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~15) and low carbohydrate content (~4.2 g per 100 g) with high lean protein from venison
type2_diabetes.guidance: Black pepper deer meat is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low GI of approximately 15 and minimal carbohydrate load; the high lean protein content (24.5 g per 100 g) supports satiety and glycaemic stability, though sodium from the sauce should be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (2.4 g per 100 g) and elevated sodium (~520 mg per 100 g); venison is leaner than most red meats
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Venison is a relatively lean red meat with lower saturated fat than beef or pork, making it a preferable red meat choice for cardiovascular health; however, the black pepper sauce contributes significant sodium and some saturated fat, so portion control and reduced-sauce options are advisable for individuals with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Black Pepper Deer Meat is a Chinese-style stir-fried dish featuring thinly sliced venison (deer meat) cooked in a bold, aromatic black pepper sauce. The dish is prepared by marinating venison slices in soy sauce and cornstarch, then stir-frying at high heat in a wok with garlic, onion, butter or oil, and a generous amount of cracked black pepper, finished with oyster sauce and sometimes a touch of sugar for balance. The result is tender, slightly gamey venison with a pungent, spicy-savoury coating and a glossy sauce. In Singapore, this dish appears on Chinese seafood and zi char restaurant menus as a premium game meat option, often priced higher than equivalent beef or pork dishes due to the relative scarcity of venison. Nutritionally, venison is a lean red meat rich in protein (approximately 24.5 g per 100 g), iron (3.8 mg), zinc (3.2 mg), and B vitamins including B12 (2.1 mcg), making it nutritionally dense. The fat content is moderate (7.8 g per 100 g) with a relatively favourable saturated-to-unsaturated fat ratio compared to beef. The primary nutritional concern is sodium from the sauce components (~520 mg per 100 g). The glycaemic index is very low (~15), making this dish appropriate for blood sugar management. Clinically, it is a suitable high-protein option for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or seeking lean red meat alternatives, with sodium monitoring recommended for those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease.

confidence_reason: No direct composite dish entry for black pepper deer meat exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy venison (raw) with cooking-loss and sauce-component adjustments. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed international GI tables. Confidence is medium rather than low because venison nutrient data from USDA is well-established and the adaptation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central venison entry and is not reliably estimated for this preparation. All other micronutrients with -1 values are not applicable here as they have been populated from USDA data where available. Nutrient values represent the cooked composite dish (venison + black pepper sauce) per 100 g edible portion. Cooking loss of approximately 25% moisture was applied to raw venison values. Sodium is elevated due to soy sauce and oyster sauce contributions and is the primary dietary concern for sodium-sensitive individuals. The cuisine_tags in the input JSON listed 'Seafood Dish', 'Crab', and 'Lobster', which are inconsistent with a deer meat dish; these tags appear to be erroneous metadata from the source restaurant's general cuisine classification (Riverfront Seafood) and have not been carried forward. GI set to 15 as an analogous estimate for a low-carbohydrate cooked meat dish per Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables; gi_category is low (GI ≤ 55). Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as (15 × 10.5 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 1.6.
